ASK-PrEP: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Stepped-Care PrEP Navigation Intervention for Sexual and Gender Minority Individuals with Substance Use Disorders

CHERISH Weill Cornell Biostatistics Internship Project

Biostatistician: Jordyn Begay and Adanya Bailey

Principal Investigator: Sean M. Murphy

July 21, 2026

Background Info

Background: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) is a highly effective strategy for preventing HIV infection among individuals at high risk of acquisition. However, despite its effectiveness, PrEP initiation, adherence, and persistence remain suboptimal, particularly among transgender women (TW) and men who have sex with men (MSM) who use substances. Substance use has been associated with reduced engagement across the PrEP care continuum, poorer health outcomes, and increased healthcare utilization, highlighting the need for interventions that improve PrEP uptake and sustained use in these populations.

This study evaluated the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of the Assistance Services Knowledge–PrEP (ASK-PrEP) intervention, a PrEP navigation program incorporating Short Message Service (SMS) support within a stepped-care randomized controlled trial. Participants who did not respond to the initial ASK-PrEP intervention were re-randomized to receive either ASK-PrEP plus contingency management (CM) or CM alone. The objective was to identify the most effective and cost-effective strategy for improving engagement across the PrEP care continuum, including initiation, adherence, and persistence.

Aims

  1. Describe the baseline characteristics of the overall study cohort and compare participant characteristics by initial randomization assignment (ASK-PrEP vs. Standard of Care [SOC])
  2. Evaluate the association between initial randomization assignment (ASK-PrEP vs. SOC) and PrEP initiation at 3 months, as well as healthcare costs using logistic regression and two-part cost models.
  3. Evaluate the association between second-stage treatment assignment (ASK-PrEP + CM vs. CM alone) and PrEP adherence at 6, 9, and 12 months, as well as healthcare costs using logistic regression and two-part cost models.

2. Treatment Initiation and Cost Outcomes of ASK PrEP vs. SOC

Bivariate Hypothesis Testing

Bivariate hypothesis tests were performed to compare treatment initiation success and healthcare costs between the two intervention groups prior to adjusted regression analyses. This table presents the unadjusted comparison of PrEP initiation at 3 months and total healthcare costs between participants randomized to the ASK-PrEP intervention and those receiving SOC.

Characteristic Overall
N = 1521		 ASK PrEP
N = 1131		 SOC
N = 391		 p-value2
PrEP Treatment Initiation Success 48 (47%) 42 (56%) 6 (22%) 0.003
    Unknown 50 38 12
Total Cost at 3 Months


0.2
    Mean (SD) 3,105 (11,722) 2,609 (11,215) 4,345 (13,010)
    Median (Q1, Q3) 0 (0, 760) 0 (0, 0) 0 (0, 815)
    Min, Max 0, 78,154 0, 78,154 0, 68,150
    Unknown 40 33 7
1 n (%)
2 Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Wilcoxon rank sum test

Interpretation: 56% of participants in the ASK-PREP arm successfully initiated PrEP by the 3-month visit, whereas only 22% of participants in the standard of care arm had (p=0.003). Healthcare costs did not significantly differ.

Statistical Models

Treatment Initiation: Logistic Regression

A multivariable logistic regression model was used to evaluate the association between initial randomization assignment (ASK-PrEP vs. SOC) and PrEP initiation at 3 months, when controlling for covariates. The model adjusted for insurance status, income, living situation, lifetime injection drug use, and race/ethnicity. Adjusted odds ratios (ORs), 95% confidence intervals (CIs), and p-values are reported.

Characteristic OR 95% CI p-value
Randomization Assignment 1


    SOC — —
    ASK PrEP 4.79 1.57, 17.0 0.009
Insurance


    Commercial — —
    Public 1.22 0.27, 5.74 0.8
    Other/Uninsured 2.31 0.32, 19.0 0.4
Income


    <$12,500 — —
    $12,500-$30,000 0.99 0.28, 3.52 >0.9
    >$30,000 1.71 0.39, 7.83 0.5
Living situation


    Stable housing — —
    Unhoused 0.80 0.22, 2.78 0.7
    Unstable housing 1.11 0.31, 4.10 0.9
Lifetime injection drug use 0.22 0.07, 0.65 0.008
Race/ethnicity


    Caucasian/White — —
    African American/Black 0.33 0.07, 1.46 0.2
    Hispanic/Latinx 1.35 0.32, 5.85 0.7
    Other 1.05 0.25, 4.40 >0.9
Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, OR = Odds Ratio

Interpretation: After adjusting for insurance status, income, living situation, lifetime injection drug use, and race/ethnicity, participants randomized to the ASK-PrEP intervention had 4.89 times the odds of initiating PrEP at 3 months compared with participants receiving Standard of Care (OR = 4.89, 95% CI: 1.60–17.5, p = 0.008). Additionally, participants with a lifetime history of injection drug use had significantly lower odds of PrEP initiation than those without a history of injection drug use (OR = 0.23, 95% CI: 0.07–0.67, p = 0.009).

Cost : Two-Part Model

A two-part model was used to evaluate the association between initial randomization assignment (ASK-PrEP vs. SOC) and total healthcare costs at 3 months. The first part of the model used logistic regression to estimate the probability of incurring any healthcare costs, while the second part used a Gamma regression with a log link to model the amount of healthcare costs among participants with non-zero costs. Adjusted marginal effects were estimated to quantify the difference in healthcare costs between treatment groups.

Variable Marginal difference in cost P-value
ASK PrEP - SOC -1256.73 (-6513.53, 4000.07) 0.6394

Interpretation: After adjusting for insurance status, there was no statistically significant difference in 3-month healthcare costs between participants randomized to ASK-PrEP and those receiving Standard of Care (adjusted marginal difference = -$3,122.63; 95% CI: -$15,748.30 to $9,503.04; p = 0.628). These findings suggest that the ASK-PrEP intervention was not associated with increased healthcare costs relative to Standard of Care.

3. Treatment Initiation and Cost Outcome of ASK PrEP + CM vs. CM (6, 9 , and 12 Months)

Bivariate Hypothesis Testing

This table presents the unadjusted comparison of PrEP maintenance at 6, 9, and 12 months and cumulative healthcare costs among participants re-randomized to ASK-PrEP + Contingency Management (CM) or CM alone. These results summarize the unadjusted differences in PrEP maintenance and healthcare costs between the two second-stage treatment groups prior to adjusted logistic regression and two-part cost model analyses.

1 n (%)
2 Fisher’s exact test; Wilcoxon rank sum test

Interpretation There were no statistically significant differences in PrEP maintenance between participants randomized to ASK-PrEP + CM and those receiving CM alone at 6, 9, or 12 months (p = 0.2, 0.6, and >0.9, respectively). Similarly, cumulative healthcare costs did not differ significantly between treatment groups at any follow-up time point, suggesting that adding ASK-PrEP to CM was not associated with increased healthcare costs.

Statistical Models

Treatment Initiation: Logistic Regression

A multivariable logistic regression model was used to evaluate the association between second-stage treatment assignment (ASK-PrEP + Contingency Management [CM] vs. CM alone) and PrEP maintenance at 6, 9, and 12 months. The 6-month model adjusted for insurance status, income, living situation, lifetime injection drug use, and race/ethnicity. Due to sparse outcome data at later follow-up, the 9-month model excluded race/ethnicity because of unstable coefficient estimates, and the 12-month model adjusted only for lifetime injection drug use because of the limited number of PrEP maintenance events. Adjusted odds ratios (ORs), 95% confidence intervals (CIs), and p-values are reported.

Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, OR = Odds Ratio

Interpretation: After adjustment for insurance status, income, living situation, lifetime injection drug use, and race/ethnicity, second-stage treatment assignment (ASK-PrEP + Contingency Management [CM] vs. CM alone) was not significantly associated with PrEP maintenance at 6 months (OR = 1.90, 95% CI: 0.16–24.4, p = 0.60). Due to unstable coefficient estimates resulting from sparse outcome data, race/ethnicity was excluded from the 9-month model. At 9 months, there remained no significant association between second-stage treatment assignment and PrEP maintenance (OR = 0.34, 95% CI: 0.03–2.82, p = 0.30). Because of the limited number of PrEP maintenance events at 12 months, the 12-month model was simplified to adjust only for lifetime injection drug use. Similarly, no significant association between second-stage treatment assignment and PrEP maintenance was observed at 12 months (OR = 2.36, 95% CI: 0.19–56.5, p = 0.50).

Cost : Two-Part Model

A longitudinal two-part model was used to compare total healthcare costs between participants assigned to ASK-PrEP + Contingency Management (CM) and those assigned to CM alone across the 6-, 9-, and 12-month follow-up periods. The first part modeled the probability of incurring any healthcare costs using logistic regression, while the second part modeled the amount of cost among participants with nonzero costs using a Gamma regression with a log link. Because of the small sample size, the model adjusted only for lifetime injection drug use history and included treatment-by-timepoint interaction terms to estimate adjusted marginal differences in cost at each follow-up time point.

Variable Marginal difference in cost P-value
ASK PrEP + CM - CM alone -5598.62 (-14220.28, 3023.04) 0.2031
ASK PrEP + CM - CM alone -1972.54 (-5165.23, 1220.15) 0.2259
ASK PrEP + CM - CM alone -12850.66 (-32285.36, 6584.05) 0.1950

Interpretation: After adjusting for lifetime injection drug use history, participants assigned to ASK-PrEP + Contingency Management (CM) did not incur significantly different healthcare costs compared with those assigned to CM alone at 6, 9, or 12 months. While the estimated marginal costs were consistently lower for the ASK-PrEP + CM group, the differences were not statistically significant (6 months: p = 0.239; 9 months: p = 0.878; 12 months: p = 0.250). These findings suggest that adding ASK-PrEP to contingency management was not associated with increased healthcare costs over the follow-up period

Figures:

Conclusions

  1. Participants randomized to the ASK-PrEP intervention had significantly greater odds of successful PrEP initiation at 3 months compared with those receiving Standard of Care (SOC), after adjusting for baseline demographic and clinical characteristics.

  2. Among participants who were re-randomized at 6 months, ASK-PrEP + Contingency Management (CM) did not significantly improve PrEP maintenance at 6, 9, or 12 months compared with CM alone after adjustment for relevant baseline covariates.

  3. Healthcare costs did not differ significantly between treatment groups in either the initial treatment comparison (ASK-PrEP vs. SOC) or the second-stage treatment comparison (ASK-PrEP + CM vs. CM alone), suggesting that the interventions were not associated with increased healthcare costs.

Overall, the findings suggest that ASK-PrEP may improve short-term PrEP initiation without increasing healthcare costs. However, among participants requiring a second-stage intervention, ASK-PrEP + CM did not significantly improve long-term PrEP maintenance compared with CM alone, indicating that additional strategies may be needed to support sustained PrEP use.

Note: This study was terminated as a result of recent federal funding cuts affecting research focused on gender and sexual minority populations. The abrupt discontinuation of funding led to the dissolution of the study’s analysis team before the collected data could be fully analyzed. The work completed this summer by this student research group is helping ensure that valuable insights are still generated from the available data, enabling actionable findings that otherwise may not have been realized.

Division of Biostatistics

Department of Population Health Sciences

Jordyn Begay and Adanya Bailey
